coredata.dc:title: Boronate-based fluorescent carbon dot for rapid and selectively bacterial sensing by luminescence off/on system
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Pharmaceutical and Biomedical Analysis
coredata.dc:creator: Choi, Cheong A
Mazrad, Zihnil Adha Islamy
Lee, Gibaek
In, Insik
Lee, Kang Dae
Park, Sung Young
coredata.dc:description: Boronic acid, which can bind chemo-selectively and reversibly to diols, could be used for the early detection of bacteria through its affinity-binding reaction with diol groups on the bacterial cell wall. Herein, we describe the use of a diol-modified fluorescent probe (DYE) conjugated to a nanosensor consisting of phenylboronic acid-functionalized fluorescent carbon dot (FCD) to allow quenching via the Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) process. Phenylboronic acid is well-known for its preferential affinity for diol-containing molecules through cyclic ester bond formation. Therefore, in the presence of glucose-containing bacteria, the DYE in the cyclic ester form will be released from the FCD and replaced by the bacterial cell forming a new cyclic boronate ester bond with the nanoparticle, inducing recovery of the fluorescence. Quantitatively, the system’s detection performance at various bacterial concentrations (101–107 CFU/mL) reached ∼100% after 60 min, indicating that the high binding affinity of the diol moeity on the peptidoglycan (sugar)-rich bacteria was enough to displace the DYE from the boronic acid-functionalized FCD platform. Our facile and tunable fluorescence switch-on system was tested for its ability to detect bacteria in water from a contaminated river. Incredibly, the system was most successful in detecting bacteria in the contaminated river water, thus proving it to be a less expensive and more robust affinity biosensor for the detection of contaminating pathogens in various chemoselective ligand-based environments.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Phenylboronic acid
Bacteria detection
Fluorescence switch-On
Fluorescent carbon dot
Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET)
Dye

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of carbonized pluronic-grafted poly(dimethylaminoethyl methacrylate)–quaternized boronic acid (FCD)
Preparation of the diol-modified fluorescent probe-conjugated FCD
Bacterial cell imaging by SEM and confocal microscopy
Development and chemical analysis of D-FCD biosensor
Bacteria detection performances of D-FCD biosensor
Cyclic voltammetry analysis of bacteria detection

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Ministry of Trade, Industry and Energy

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research was supported by Grant No. 10062079 from the Ministry of Trade, Industry & Energy (MOTIE) and the Basic Science Research Program through the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) funded by the Ministry of Education ( NRF-2017R1A2B2002365 and 2018R1A6A1A03023788 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) The routes of D-FCD synthesis. (b) Illustration of D-FCD detection of bacteria in a solution via fluorescence microscopy and cyclic voltammetry.
(a) 1H-NMR and (b) UV–vis spectra of B-PgP, FCD, and D-FCD in deionized water (1 mg/mL). Wide-scan and narrow-scan C 1s and S 2p XPS spectra of (c) FCD and (d) D-FCD.

Absorbance (330 nm) of DYE in solution, for quantitation of the D-FCD performance in detecting (a) S. aureus and (a) E. coli. Quantitative analysis of the bacterial detection performance in reference to the plotted calibration curves of DYE, for (c) S. aureus and (d) E. coli. The bacterial concentrations were 101, 103, 105, and 107 CFU/mL, and incubation was for 60 min. Confocal images of the centrifugation-obtained DYE solution after incubation of D-FCDs with various concentrations of (e) S. aureus and (f) E. coli (101, 103, 105, and 107 CFU/mL) for 60 min.

Photoluminescence signals of D-FCDs (Ex: 380 nm and Em: 400 nm) from the centrifugation-obtained cell pellet after incubation of the nanoparticles with different concentrations of (a) S. aureus and (b) E. coli (101, 103, 105, and 107 CFU/mL) for 0–60 min. (c) Confocal images of D-FCD detection of 107 CFU/mL of S. aureus and E. coli at various incubation time periods.

Cyclic voltammograms of D-FCD-modified screen-printed carbon electrodes incubated with various concentrations of (a) S. aureus and (b) E. coli, and at various incubation times of (c) S. aureus and (d) E. coli adhesion.

Effectiveness of the D-FCDs for bacterial detection in contaminated river water. Fluorescence microscopy images of D-FCDs incubated with (a) Deionized distiled water (DDW) and (b) contaminated water for 60 min.

head.ce:title: Boronate-based fluorescent carbon dot for rapid and selectively bacterial sensing by luminescence off/on system

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemical and Biological Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Polymer Science and Engineering, Korea National University of Transportation, Chungju 380-702, Republic of Korea

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Polymer Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Otolaryngology–Head and Neck Surgery, Kosin University College of Medicine, Busan 49267, Republic of Korea

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Otolaryngology–Head and Neck Surgery

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Cooresponding author at: Department of Chemical and Biological Engineering, Korea National University of Transportation, Chungju, 380-702, Republic of Korea.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemical and Biological Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Boronic acid, which can bind chemo-selectively and reversibly to diols, could be used for the early detection of bacteria through its affinity-binding reaction with diol groups on the bacterial cell wall. Herein, we describe the use of a diol-modified fluorescent probe (DYE) conjugated to a nanosensor consisting of phenylboronic acid-functionalized fluorescent carbon dot (FCD) to allow quenching via the Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) process. Phenylboronic acid is well-known for its preferential affinity for diol-containing molecules through cyclic ester bond formation. Therefore, in the presence of glucose-containing bacteria, the DYE in the cyclic ester form will be released from the FCD and replaced by the bacterial cell forming a new cyclic boronate ester bond with the nanoparticle, inducing recovery of the fluorescence. Quantitatively, the system’s detection performance at various bacterial concentrations (101–107 CFU/mL) reached ∼100% after 60 min, indicating that the high binding affinity of the diol moeity on the peptidoglycan (sugar)-rich bacteria was enough to displace the DYE from the boronic acid-functionalized FCD platform. Our facile and tunable fluorescence switch-on system was tested for its ability to detect bacteria in water from a contaminated river. Incredibly, the system was most successful in detecting bacteria in the contaminated river water, thus proving it to be a less expensive and more robust affinity biosensor for the detection of contaminating pathogens in various chemoselective ligand-based environments.

ce:section.ce:para: Microorganisms, especially bacteria, have become fundamental research targets for many microbiologists in terms of acquiring information about their affects and contaminating processes, as well as evaluating the effectiveness of antimicrobial agents [1–3]. One of the traditional methods for detecting pathogenic bacteria is the use of affinity biosensors through their interaction with immobilized biological components, such as antibodies, DNA, and receptor proteins on the transducer surface and target molecules [4,5]. Despite their high sensitivity, selectivity, and low detection limit of bacteria, biological-sensing elements have some limitations, including low stability, high cost, and laborious techniques. Chemoselective ligands have been regarded as alternatives to biological receptors, owing to their greater resistance to chemical biodegradation, better determination of the kinetic parameters of affinity interactions, and cheaper and faster analytical requirements [6–8].

The bacterial cell surface comprises polysaccharide components such as lipopolysaccharide, teichoic acid, and teichuronic acid, which consist of diols groups that might be either associated with other surface components or totally dissociated from the microbial cell [9–11]. Through these surface polysaccharides, bacteria can recognize and complex with other molecules by strong chemical bonding [12,13]. Boronic acid has been proven to be able to form complexes with compounds containing vicinal diol groups (boronate ester bond) under basic conditions through simple mixing, which have been extensively used for carbohydrate sensors and transporters [14]. Furthermore, the boronate ester bond with 1,2- or 1,3-diols is reversible, and therefore has been widely applied to functionalize biosensors for the recognition of saccharides and glycosylated biomolecules on bacterial surfaces though surface plasmon resonance, quartz crystal microbalance, and electrochemical methods [15–17]. However, there are no detection methods using fluorescent carbon dot (FCD) in a fluorescence quenching switch-on system activated through chemoselectivity of diol-containing compounds on the bacterium cell wall.

The staining of bacteria with fluorescent dyes (e.g., propidium iodide, ethidium bromide, and SYTO BC) in flow cytometry has been successfully investigated in the routine assessment of bacterial counts [18,19]. However, these techniques require expensive equipment. In our previous work, a new class of fluorescent materials, FCD, proved useful in life science applications, particularly for bacterial detection using ionic complex and hydrophobic interaction between cationic FCD and anionic phosphatase groups on the cell surface [11,12]. The tiny size, excellent photoluminescent properties, and high biocompatibility of the FCD (obtained by a simple condensation reaction as well as carbonization and dehydration processes) give them great potential for application in various fields of biological research. In addition, in order to acquire accurate results in bacterial detection, quantitative analysis is extremely important, but was limited in our previous research. Therefore, in this present study, our FCD system was further improved to allow for a more accurate determination of the number or concentration of bacteria captured.

With these aims in mind, we developed a diol-modified fluorescent probe (DYE) in this study, to quench the carbonized Pluronic-grafted poly(dimethylaminoethyl methacrylate)–quaternized boronic acid by the Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) mechanism. Upon the addition of Escherichia coli (E. coli) and Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus) to the DYE-conjugated FCD (D-FCD) solution, the diol groups of polysaccharides on the bacterial cell surface form new cyclic boronate ester bonds with the FCDs and replace the DYE molecules. Consequently, the DYE molecules are released into solution and remain in the supernatant after centrifugation, while the bacteria-bound FCDs are precipitated as the cell pellet. Quenching of the FCDs on the bacterial cell surface restores their higher fluorescence intensity in a manner similar to the capillary electrophoresis–laser-induced fluorescence phenomenon [20]. Besides exploring the fluorescence changes, the released DYE also allows for quantitative analysis through measurement of its UV–vis spectroscopy absorbance. This study intended to test the usefulness of cyclic voltammetry (CV) in monitoring the initial bacterial binding through detection of the electron transfer between the bacterial surface and the electrode surface. The electrode surface coverage by bacteria was also estimated from cyclic voltammograms to validate the potential of CV as the quantitative detection method of bacterial attachment. This tunable platform may be suitable as a confirmatory test, as it displayed high sensitivity and chemical specificity for bacteria detection, in addition to being relatively simple and inexpensive.

Pluronic F-127, 2-(dimethylamino)ethyl methacrylate (DMA), 4-chlorophenylboronic acid, anhydrous toluene, hexane, ethanol, diethyl ether, 5-(2-aminoethylamino)-naphthalene-1-sulfonic acid, Trizma base, potassium ferricyanide (K3[Fe(CN)6]), potassium chloride (KCl), Trizma HCl, glyceraldehyde, dialysis membrane (Mw = 500–1000), and concentrated sulfuric acid (H2SO4) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (Seoul, Korea). The DYE molecules were synthesized as described in a previous report [21–23].

Proton NMR spectra were recorded using a Bruker AVANCE III 400 MHz spectrometer with dimethyl sulfoxide (d-DMSO) as the solvent. Absorption spectra were acquired on an Optizen 2120UV spectrophotometer (Mecasys, Yuseong-gu, South Korea). Fluorescence properties were analyzed by means of an L550B luminescence spectrometer from Perkin-Elmer (Waltham, MA, USA). Field-emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM) micrographs were obtained using a JEOL JSM-6700 F SEM (Tokyo, Japan). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were acquired using an ESCALAB apparatus (Omicron Vacuumphysik GmbH, Taunustein, Germany) and a PHI Quantera-II system (Ulvac-PHI, Chigasaki, Japan). Analysis of the system’s bacteria detection performance was conducted under an LSM 510 confocal laser-scanning microscope (CLSM; Carl Zeiss, Oberkochen, Germany) using 405-, 488-, and 543-nm emission filters at 20× magnification. Fluorescence lifetimes were measured using a NanoLED laser light source (Horiba Jobin Yvon NanoLog spectrophotometer; Horiba Korea Ltd, Seoul, Korea) at 375-nm excitation wavelength, and the data were fitted by a multi-exponential decay model. The samples for the fluorescence lifetime measurements were prepared by dissolving the FCD system in an aqueous solution at very low concentrations (1.0 mg/mL). Screen-printed carbon electrodes (SPCEs) were purchased from EGTechnology (Seoul, Korea) and used for the CV analyses using the μStat 4000 P Multi Potentiostat (DropSens, Asturias, Spain).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of carbonized pluronic-grafted poly(dimethylaminoethyl methacrylate)–quaternized boronic acid (FCD)
ce:section.ce:para: The method used for the synthesis of Pluronic-grafted poly(dimethylaminoethyl methacrylate)–quaternized boronic acid (B-PgP) was as described in a previous report [13,26]. To fabricate the FCDs, 1 g of B-PgP was dissolved in 5 mL of deionized Millipore water at 25 °C. Then, 10 mL of 36 N H2SO4 was added, and the mixture was stirred for 1 min. The final reaction mixture was then added to 185 mL of double-deionized water (DDW) and prepared for dialysis in water using a membrane (MWCO: 1000). Finally, the product was freeze-dried and collected.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of the diol-modified fluorescent probe-conjugated FCD
ce:section.ce:para: The B-PgP-functionalized FCD (100 mg) and DYE (0.005 mg/mL) were dissolved in 10 mL of Tris-buffered saline (TBS) at pH 12.0. The solution was allowed to react for 24 h at room temperature to obtain D-FCDs. The reaction solution was then dialyzed (MWCO: 500–1000) for 24 h and freeze-dried.

To detect bacteria via the boronate ester bonding behavior, we used S. aureus (gram-positive, strain ATCC 25,323) and E. coli (gram-negative, strain ATCC 25,922) as the test pathogens, cultured in 50 mL of MRS and LB media, respectively, at 37 °C for 24 h. After that, bacterial cells were harvested by centrifugation, and the pellet was resuspended in PBS 7.4 buffer. Then, various concentrations of each bacterial cell solution (107, 105, 103, or 101 colony-forming units (CFU/mL) were incubated with 1 mg/mL of D-FCDs. At different interval times, the bacteria-bound nanoparticles were pelleted by centrifugation and washed 3 times. The cell pellet labeled with D-FCD was resuspended in PBS and examined on an L550B luminescence spectrometer (Excitation wavelength: 380 nm, Emission wavelength: 400 nm), as well as under the LSM 510 CLSM to verify the performance of the D-FCDs for detection of bacteria [11,13]. At the same time, the supernatant from the centrifugation was investigated by UV–vis spectroscopy to measure the absorbance of the DYE molecules released into solution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Bacterial cell imaging by SEM and confocal microscopy
ce:section.ce:para: For SEM imaging, the bacterial suspensions cultured as described above were centrifuged and washed consecutively with 25%, 50%, 75%, and 99% ethanol. A few microliters of bacteria—either untreated or treated with nanoparticles—was spread on a silicon wafer, which was then dried for 3–4 h and used to record SEM micrographs. For confocal imaging, the cell pellet and supernatant obtained by centrifugation were respectively resuspended with PBS (pH 7.4) and then spread over a cover slide. The bacterial cells were examined using the LSM 510 CLSM (magnification, 20×).

To study bacterial attachment effects on the D-FCD-modified surface, CV analysis was performed. Commercial SPCE strips were modified with the synthesized D-FCD by a simple drop-coating method. In brief, 10 μL of the prepared D-FCD dispersion (1 mg/mL) was dropped onto the surface of the working electrode of the SPCE. Then, the SPCEs were dried in an oven at 90 °C for 10 min. Thereafter, the SPCEs were incubated in the bacterial solution under the same concentrations and conditions as used in the previous experiments. The SPCEs were then washed with PBS and dried at room temperature. The D-FCD-altered SPCEs were characterized by CV, using a μStat 4000 P Multi Potentiostat with 10 mM K3[(FeCN)6] as the electrolyte and 100 mM KCl as the supporting electrolyte. CV curves were scanned in the range from ―0.2 to +0.7 V at a scan rate of 50 mV/s.

For selectivity test of the established sensor, different samples including monosaccharide (Glucose) (1 mg/mL), disaccharide (Lactose) (1 mg/mL), and epithelial model mammalian cell line- Madin-Darby Canine Kidney (MDCK) (1 × 105 cells/mL) were incubated with D-FCD (1 mg/mL) for specific incubation time. The solutions then were examined by L550B luminescence spectrometer at an excitation wavelength of 380 nm and emission wavelength at 400 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Development and chemical analysis of D-FCD biosensor
ce:section.ce:para: A diol-sensing component was used in this study for bacteria detection based on the competitive binding affinity of the cyclic boronate ester bond [24,25]. In the present of the DYE molecules, our FCDs made of carbonized Pluronic-grafted polyDMA–quaternized boronic acid, prepared as shown in Scheme 1 a, are quenched as a result of the electron-receiving ability of DYE via the FRET mechanism. Upon addition of the bacteria into our system, the boronic acid in the DMA backbone forms new boronate ester bond with the diol-containing glucose on the bacterial cell surface. The boron moiety becomes negatively charged after glucose binding and the ability of the DYE to quench the FCDs is weakened, resulting in release of the probe molecule and increase of the fluorescence intensity of the nanoparticle. The binding of FCDs and glucose-containing bacteria via the boronate ester bond thus allows us to detect the bacteria through the changes of fluorescence, while the DYE release allows for quantitative sensing through measurement of the absorbance by UV–vis spectroscopy. In addition, these interactions could be rapidly identified with high accuracy by the current signal generated by CV. The technique applied to detect the bacteria was accomplished using facile centrifugation, as shown in Scheme 1b.

The chemical formation of D-FCD was characterized by 1H-NMR and UV–vis spectroscopy (Fig. 1 ). The intense peaks at 0.8 ppm [3H, C − CH3], 1.0 ppm [2H, −CH2], 4.1–4.2 ppm [2H, −O − CH2], 2.7–2.8 ppm [2H, N − CH2], and 2.2–2.3 ppm [3H, −CH3] indicated that the DMA had been grafted to the Pluronic backbone [26,37]. We also found integral peaks at around 7.2 − 8.0 ppm, which confirmed that the aromatic groups of boronic acid were retained even after carbonization. The peak at 5.2–5.3 ppm was assigned to the boronate ester bond between boronic acid and the diol group, confirming the conjugation of DYE to the FCD [27]. Fig. 1b shows UV–vis spectra of B-PgP, FCD, and D-FCD at the concentration of 1 mg/mL in water. The sharp peak at around 260 nm was attributed to the amphiphilic boronic acid compound, and the appearance of the peak at 350 nm from the D-FCD sample indicated formation of the boronate ester bond between the diol and boronic acid species [28]. The broader absorption peaks of the FCD system compared with that of B-PgP and D-FCD implies that the π–π* electron transitions produced from acid treatment can enhance photoelectric properties and allow the nanoparticles to act as chromophores [29]. In addition, we also observed the elemental binding from XPS spectra of FCD and D-FCD (Fig. 1c and 1d). In the narrow scan of the XPS spectra, we highlighted two elements (i.e., C 1 s and S 2p) for both before and after DYE conjugation. The XPS narrow scan of the C 1 s spectra of FCD and D-FCD depicted peaks at 283.4, 284.5, 285.5, and 288.4 eV, corresponding to CC, CC, CO, and CO, respectively, with slight increase of the CC binding energy due to DYE conjugation. On the D-FCD spectrum, the S 2p peak at 168.6 eV was assigned to the C-SO3H bond on the DYE (Scheme 1) [30,31], which was not found on the FCD spectrum, reflecting the successful chemical construction and structural formula of the cyclic boronate ester bond.

After carbonization, such materials containing carbon often conceive sp2- and sp3-hybridized carbon atoms, which allows them to generate the excitation-dependent tunable fluorescence profiles [32]. We hypothesized that when the FCD system is conjugated with DYE via the boron-diol bond, the emission of the nanoparticles would be quenched by the FRET mechanism [33]. As shown in Fig. S1a, the expected excitation-dependent fluorescence emissions were seen for FCD at various excitation wavelengths, whereas the fluorescence properties were not evident after DYE conjugation (Fig. S1b), indicating the quenching effect by the FRET process. The quenching effect was also investigated according to fluorescence lifetime for both system designs. The results revealed a decrease in the decay time of FCD, from 10.9 ns to 3.65 ns (Fig. S1c), implying that the DYE molecule can receive the electron transferred from the FCD emission process upon light absorption [34]. After that, the bacterial detection ability of D-FCD was confirmed via fluorescent signals obtained after incubation D-FCD with bacterial cells (Fig. S1d and S1e). At different bacterial cell concentrations, the fluorescent intensities were recovered due to the formation of new boronate ester bond between FCD and diol-containing sugar on the bacterial membrane, which subsequently released DYE. Moreover, direct evidence of the interaction between the bacterial cell and D-FCD was confirmed via size analysis by dynamic light scattering (DLS). The particle size of D-FCD, which was found to be around 90 nm in the TEM images, increased to 1188 and 1578 nm in the DLS histogram after binding with S. aureus and E. coli, respectively (Fig. S1f and S1g), confirming the interaction between the bacteria and D-FCDs. Therefore, these results revealed that our system has the potential to detect the presence of bacteria via the fluorescence switch-on system by employing the cyclic boronate ester bond formation ability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Bacteria detection performances of D-FCD biosensor
ce:section.ce:para: Detection was made possible by the competitive binding between the DYE molecule and its cyclic boronate ester bond from phenylboronic acid and the diol-containing glucose in bacteria (creating a new cyclic boronate ester bond) for FCD, since glucose has a stronger affinity to boron sites than do diol dyes [21]. The bacteria changed the boronate ester bond between the boronic acid-functionalized FCD and DYE [35], hence prompting the release of DYE into the medium solution; meanwhile, the FCDs newly bonded to bacteria are located in the pellet after centrifugation. The cell pellet was analyzed by photoluminescence spectroscopy, whereas the DYE-containing supernatant was analyzed by UV–vis spectroscopy as a function of incubation time with different bacteria concentrations (101, 103, 105, and 107 CFU/mL) and a fixed concentration of nanoparticles. The absorbance of DYE in the solution gradually increased with increasing incubation time for both E. coli and S. aureus, confirming that DYE had been replaced by the bacteria (Fig. 2 a and b). The absorbance (A) values exhibited a linear response towards released DYE within a concentration (C) range from 0.01 to 0.13 mg/mL, following the linear regression equation of A = 11.27*C (R2 = 0.9984358) (Fig. S3). It is suggested that the numbers of bacteria could be quantified from the respective numbers of DYE released after incubation. Hence, we then quantitatively calculated the bacteria detection performance based on the absorbance values of released DYE; this test essentially determines the detection limit of the D-FCD system for the bacteria solution. The ability of our D-FCD system to capture/detect the bacteria reached ∼100% after 60 min incubation with the various bacteria concentrations (101–107 CFU/mL) (Fig. 2c and d). In other words, ∼100% of the nanoparticles created boronate ester bonds with the glucose-containing bacteria and released 100% of the DYE into solution, indicating the high sensitivity of D-FCD in sensing wide ranges of bacteria concentration. In addition, we investigated confocal images of DYE release from D-FCD before and after incubation with the bacteria. The fluorescence of DYE increased gradually from 0 to 60 min, with the brightest emission at 405 nm shown after 60 min of D-FCD incubation with the highest concentration (107 CFU/mL) of E. coli and S. aureus (Fig. 2e and f).

For the photoluminescence analysis, bacteria suspended in PBS (pH 7.4) were incubated with the D-FCDs for 60 min, following which, spectra were obtained at 380 nm excitation wavelength (Fig. 3 ). The bacteria detection performance of the D-FCD system was determined from the fluorescence response at various incubation times. The fluorescence intensity of the nanoparticles was dramatically enhanced after incubation with either E. coli or S. aureus, increasing with longer incubation time, indicating the localization of FCDs around the bacterial cell surfaces. The system showed ability to detect the presence of bacteria with short response time within early minutes of experiment. The increase of fluorescence signal was recognized right after first minutes of incubation with D-FCD and continuously increased until the limit of the sensor was found to be as low as 101 CFU/mL in both and S. aureus (Fig. 3a) and E. coli (Fig. 3b) with response time around 30 min. The proportional increase in fluorescence intensity with increasing bacteria–FCD interaction time was similar to that for the electrophoresis–laser-induced fluorescence separation method previously reported [20]. The interaction between D-FCD and bacteria caused by the presence on its surface of diol-rich molecules such as teichoic acids in Gram-positive and lipopolysaccharides (LPS) in Gram-negative bacteria. However, the binding of E. coli resulted in a slightly higher fluorescence intensity than did that of S. aureus. This is due to the fact that E. coli exposes many free diol groups on the LPS of outer membrane which can bind with boronic acid moities in the fibre membranes while in the case of Gram-positive S. aureus, with a smaller number of diol-containing sugar compounds present in the thick peptidoglycan layer, lowering fluorescence intensities [40]. It can be concluded that the fluorescence signal expressed differently for each type, specifically Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacteria in this case because the bacterial outer membrane system is complicated in its morphology and chemical composition among types, species and strains. To visualize the changes in emission of the nanoparticles at different incubation times, the D-FCDs were allowed to interact with bacteria in solution for 60 min, during which bacterial samples were collected at fixed intervals and their emission changes checked using CLSM. Similar to the results shown in Fig. 3a–c showed complete recovery of the FCD emission after 60 min of interaction with 107 CFU/mL bacteria.

We extended our studies for selective detection of glucose-based carbohydrates and other cells using D-FCDs nanosensor. As shown in Fig. S4, D-FCDs can be also used for the detection of any glucose-based carbohydrate molecules such as glucose, glucose-based carbohydrate (lactose) or monosaccharides-based sialic acids attached to mammalian cell (MDCK) surface glycoconjugates [38,39]. On the other hand, D-FCD was much enhanced the fluorescent signal when incubated with bacteria due to the presence of rich diol-containing glucose on bacterial surfaces. Therefore, fluorescence-based D-FCD based nano sensor can be used for specific detection of glucose or glucose-based carbohydrate molecules.

To further visualize the bacteria concentration-dependent emission changes of D-FCD, the functionalized nanoparticles were treated with various concentrations of both bacterial strains and CLSM images were obtained before and after 60 min incubation (Fig. S2a and S2b). With the higher bacteria concentrations, there should obviously be much more D-FCD conjugation (via the boronate ester bond) with glucose-containing bacteria than there is with a low cell concentration, and therefore the brightest fluorescence signal was indeed found for the 107 CFU/mL concentrations after 60 min of incubation. In contrast to bacteria detection using ionic complex-employing quenching mechanism [36], our unique approach focuses on bacteria detection by investigating the increase in fluorescence of FCDs. This increase is due to the recognition of diol-glucose on the bacteria surfaces by FCDs, involving both proton dissociation and boronic acid–cis-diol esterification reactions, similarly to the capillary electrophoresis–laser-induced fluorescence phenomenon used to detect bacteria [17,18]. To visualize the interaction between the bacteria and FCDs, the cell pellets obtained by centrifugation were examined by FE-SEM (Fig. S2c) [12,13]. The cell surfaces of the various concentrations of bacteria were visibly seen to be covered with FCDs after 60 min of incubation, compared with the pristine S. aureus and E. coli controls that were not incubated with nanoparticles. The number of nanoparticles adhered onto E. coli was higher than that on S. aureus, reinforcing the role of lipopolysaccharide on the outer membrane of E. coli in facilitating easier reach to the FCDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cyclic voltammetry analysis of bacteria detection
ce:section.ce:para: Additionally, the interactions between the FCDs and various concentrations of bacteria (101, 103, 105, and 107 CFU/ml) were studied by CV to investigate the effects of cell density on the attachment. The sensor strips coated with D-FCD and incubated with 107 CFU/mL bacteria showed a noticeable loss in electrochemical current signals, indicating that a large number of bacterial cells had bonded with the D-FCDs and this inhibited electron transfer along the FCD core (Fig. 4 ). In contrast, the signals for 101 CFU/mL bacterial adhesions were higher. The gradual reduction in the current signals of the D-FCD-coated sensor strips as a function of time demonstrated the complete coverage of the bacteria layer after 40 min.

Finally, to evaluate the potential application of the as-prepared FCD system for analysis of real-world samples, we tested it on contaminated water from the Dalcheon river located in Chungju, South Korea. In the confocal images, the blue emission recorded after the 60-min incubation of the contaminated water with D-FCDs was similar to the in situ experiment profiles (Fig. 5 ). In comparison, D-FCDs incubated with DDW as a control did not show any changes in emission after 60 min, indicating the absence of bacteria. Overall, the results indicated that our biosensor is able to determine the presence of bacteria in any condition, even in a complicated matrix like river water in which there are many particles.

In conclusion, a diol-modified fluorescent probe (5-(2-aminoethylamino)-naphthalene-1-sulfonic acid) was prepared to quench the carbonized Pluronic-grafted polyDMA–quaternized boronic acid by a FRET mechanism for specific interaction with phenylboronic acid, to be applied as a novel bacteria monitoring sensor based on fluorescence and CV performances. Upon the addition of E. coli or S. aureus, the fluorescence intensity of the bacteria-attached D-FCDs gradually increased, while the DYE molecules released to solution allowed quantitation of the binding. Quantitative analysis showed the system to have a detection performance of 100% for a wide concentration range of bacteria, which was verified by confocal and SEM images that showed the restoration of DYE and FCD, respectively. The attachment of bacteria to the FCD contributed to the decrease in the CV current signals, indicating the inhibition of electron transfer and the surface coverage of the electrodes by bacteria. This tunable platform is expected to be suitable as a confirmatory test, displaying high sensitivity and chemical specificity for bacteria detection, in addition to being relatively simple and inexpensive to use.
The authors state that “There are no conflicts to declare”.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was supported by Grant No. 10062079 from the Ministry of Trade, Industry & Energy (MOTIE) and the Basic Science Research Program through the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) funded by the Ministry of Education (NRF-2017R1A2B2002365 and 2018R1A6A1A03023788).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jpba.2018.06.043.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:
